color_semantics.TEAL: quantum wavefunction, exponential decay tail, transmitted amplitude, tunneling
color_semantics.CRIMSON: classical reflection, forbidden region boundary, zero-transmission expectation

metadata.note: Card 05. Exclusions: no transmission-coefficient derivation, no WKB integral, no sinh² exact formula, no tunneling-time controversy.

beats.narration_text: A classical ball thrown at a wall it cannot climb bounces back. Every time. With certainty.

card.headline: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall

beats.narration_text: Fire a ball at a wall higher than its energy. It reflects — kinetic energy converts to potential, runs out, reverses. The wall wins, always.
A quantum particle aimed at the same barrier sometimes appears on the other side. How does it get through a region it cannot enter?
Classically, if kinetic energy is zero inside the barrier, there is no motion. The particle cannot be there. The wavefunction must be zero. But it isn't.
Inside a forbidden region, Schrödinger's equation gives ψ'' = κ²ψ — a real exponential, not an oscillation. The wavefunction decays, but it does not reach zero.
The mechanism: exponential decay through the barrier.

card.headline: The amplitude passes through — decaying exponentially.

beats.narration_text: If the barrier is thin enough, some amplitude survives at the far side. That surviving tail launches an oscillating wave beyond the wall — a transmitted particle.
The transmission probability goes as e^{−2κL}. Halve the barrier width and transmission multiplies by e^{κL} — often millions of times larger.
That exponential sensitivity is not a flaw. It's the point.

card.headline: Exponential sensitivity — that is the point.

beats.narration_text: In the scanning tunneling microscope, the tip hovers one nanometer above the surface. Moving it 0.1 nm closer multiplies the tunneling current by ten. That is how the STM images individual atoms.

props.headline: In the scanning tunneling microscope, the tip hovers one nanometer above…

beats.narration_text: The same physics explains alpha decay. A helium nucleus trapped in a uranium nucleus tunnels through a Coulomb barrier — a one-page calculation predicts half-lives across 24 orders of magnitude.
The numbers: κ for a one-eV barrier.

Electron, mass 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg, barrier height V₀ − E = 1 eV = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ J. Decay constant κ = √(2mΔV)/ħ ≈ 5.1 nm⁻¹. Over 1 nm: probability density falls by e^{−10.2} ≈ 3.7×10⁻⁵. Small — but measurable by an STM.
How does a quantum particle cross a wall it cannot enter? Its amplitude decays through the barrier — and if the wall is thin, enough survives to reach the other side. QUANTUM MECHANICS.

card.headline: Its amplitude decays through — and survives.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
